text: The surgeon performs a robotic single incision inguinal hernia repair, beginning with three small stab wounds at the umbilicus for trocar placement. The laparoscope provides direct visualization as the abdomen is entered bluntly, revealing the layers of the abdominal wall. The abdomen is then insufflated for inspection of potential pathology or bilateral hernias.

The surgeon begins the robotic single incision inguinal hernia repair by making three small stab wounds at the umbilicus: one at the superior rim, one at the base, and one at the inferior rim. Trocars are placed at these sites for laparoscopic access. The abdomen is entered bluntly under direct laparoscopic visualization, allowing inspection of the abdominal wall layers as they are traversed. The abdomen is then insufflated for further inspection of potential pathology or bilateral hernias.

The surgeon places the camera port in the superior rim of the umbilicus, followed by the insertion of robotic arms one and two into the base and inferior rim. These arms are crossed at the level of the fascia for optimal flexibility. The camera port, equipped with an insufflation port, allows CO₂ insufflation. Five millimeter robotic ports are inserted through the lower two stab wounds, demonstrating the use of crossed straight instruments with long wrists for the operation.

The surgeon places the camera port in the superior rim, followed by the placement of robotic arms one and two in the base and inferior rim, respectively. These arms are crossed at the level of the fascia for optimal flexibility. The camera port, equipped with an insufflation port, replaces the initial five-millimeter port. Five-millimeter robotic ports are inserted through the lower two stab wounds, allowing the crossed instruments to function independently due to their long wrists.

The robotic arms are configured to work independently with long-jawed instruments, attached to the ports. The camera arm is positioned in the superior rim of the umbilicus, providing a 30-degree upward view of the inguinal region where dissection will occur. Arm number two is attached to the five millimeter port on the left, and arm number one is positioned to cross at the level of the fascia.

The robotic arms are attached to the ports, with the camera arm positioned in the superior rim of the umbilicus using a thirty-degree up-viewing scope. The surgeon begins the transabdominal preperitoneal operation by incising the peritoneum anterior to the inguinal defect and stripping it off the underside of the groin. The articulating wristed robotic instruments are visible, allowing for adequate separation and preventing clashing during the procedure.

The surgeon begins the transabdominal preperitoneal operation by incising the peritoneum anterior to the inguinal defect and stripping it from the underside of the groin. Articulating wristed robotic instruments are used to prevent clashing and maintain orientation. The peritoneal hernia sac is dissected off the spermatic cord, with the edge of the hernia sac clearly visible. Blunt, sharp, and cautery dissection techniques are employed, with a hook cautery available for hemostasis.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneal hernia sac from the spermatic cord using blunt and sharp dissection, with the option of using a hook cautery for hemostasis. The edge of the hernia sac is clearly visible with enhanced robotic visualization. The dissection continues up the spermatic cord to ensure the hernia sac is fully separated, creating adequate space for mesh placement.

The surgeon continues dissection of the hernia sac along the spermatic cord to ensure proper placement of the mesh and prevent curling. After clearing the area, the mesh is introduced through the larger camera port, measuring eight and a half millimeters. Parietex ProGrip mesh is used, featuring a Velcro-like backing for secure attachment to underlying structures. Needle holders serve as graspers, providing high grip strength for precise mesh handling.

The surgeon places the Parietex ProGrip mesh through the larger camera port, which measures eight and a half millimeters. The mesh features a Velcro-like backing for secure attachment to underlying structures. Needle holders are used as graspers, providing high grip strength for handling the mesh and gripping the peritoneum during dissection of the cord structures. The mesh can be sutured in place if necessary.

The surgeon uses high grip strength instruments to efficiently strip the peritoneal hernia sac from the cord structures. The final step involves closing the peritoneum with a V-lock absorbable suture, which is anchored without knot tying. Wristed robotic instruments facilitate running the suture securely, avoiding gapping and eliminating the need for tacks.

The surgeon closes the peritoneum using a V-lock absorbable suture, which features a loop on the back end to anchor the suture without knot tying. Wristed robotic instruments facilitate the running of the suture, ensuring a secure closure without gapping. The suture line is completed by backing up the V-lock suture on itself, and the suture is cut using robotic scissors. The assistant enters through the second robotic port, positioned in front of the camera.

The surgeon completes the suture line using a V-lock suture, backing it up on itself without the need for knot tying. The suture is then cut using robotic scissors, with assistance provided through the second robotic port visible in front of the camera.
